zones.label: MicroPython + ulab
zones.summary: Pure Python inference — your first ML experiments
facts: Framework: ulab (MicroPython extension — numpy-style arrays)
Boards: RP2040 (Pico), RP2350 (Pico 2), ESP32 (with ulab firmware)
Model format: custom Python code — your own kNN, decision tree, or linear regression
Model size: < 10 KB (hand-coded logic; no neural net runtime)
Inference speed: moderate — pure Python array math; ~10–100 ms per prediction
Training: on a PC using scikit-learn; export weights as Python lists
Memory needed: fits in 264 KB RAM (keep models simple)
MicroPython: import ulab.numpy as np; then matrix math for inference
Best for: simple classifiers (kNN, linear regression); first ML experiments

zones.label: TensorFlow Lite Micro
zones.summary: On-device neural networks with a fast C++ runtime
facts: Framework: TensorFlow Lite for Microcontrollers (TFLite Micro)
Boards: RP2040, RP2350 (via C/C++ port); ESP32 (with Arduino or IDF)
Model format: .tflite flatbuffer file — quantized INT8 preferred for small RAM
Model size: 10–200 KB (quantized; full float models are larger)
Inference speed: fast — C++ runtime; ~1–50 ms per image classification frame
Training: Google Colab or local PC using TensorFlow + Keras; export as .tflite
Memory needed: ~64–512 KB RAM for typical small models
MicroPython: not native — requires C extension or an Arduino build
Best for: keyword spotting, gesture recognition, small image classification

zones.label: Edge Impulse
zones.summary: End-to-end, no-code ML platform with a guided workflow
facts: Framework: Edge Impulse cloud platform + generated C++ or Arduino library
Boards: Pico, ESP32, Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense, many more (web-based)
Model format: exports a C++ library (.zip) optimized for your board
Model size: 5–100 KB (optimized and quantized automatically)
Inference speed: very fast — CMSIS-NN optimized for ARM Cortex-M; < 10 ms typical
Training: upload data on edgeimpulse.com; train in browser; auto-deploys
Memory needed: board profiler shows exact RAM/Flash usage before deploy
MicroPython: export Arduino sketch or C++ library; integrate via serial or direct port
Best for: audio classification, anomaly detection, motion recognition

quiz.question: Which approach runs inference in pure Python and is best for your first ML experiments like kNN or linear regression?
quiz.explanation: MicroPython + ulab lets you hand-code simple classifiers using numpy-style array math. Models stay under 10 KB and fit easily in the Pico's 264 KB of RAM — perfect for a first ML project.

quiz.question: Which approach uses a .tflite flatbuffer model file and a fast C++ runtime for on-device neural networks?
quiz.explanation: TensorFlow Lite Micro loads a quantized INT8 .tflite model and runs it with a fast C++ runtime (~1–50 ms per frame). It is the go-to for keyword spotting and gesture recognition.

quiz.question: Which approach lets you upload data in a browser, train a model with no code, and auto-deploy it to your board?
quiz.explanation: Edge Impulse is an end-to-end cloud platform: you upload data on edgeimpulse.com, train in the browser, and it generates a board-optimized C++ library — a complete no-code ML pipeline.

quiz.question: Which approach is the only one that works natively in MicroPython without a C extension or Arduino build?
quiz.explanation: Only MicroPython + ulab runs directly in MicroPython (import ulab.numpy as np). Both TFLite Micro and Edge Impulse require a C extension, Arduino build, or generated C++ library.

quiz.question: Which approach uses CMSIS-NN optimization for ARM Cortex-M and includes a board profiler that shows RAM and Flash usage before you deploy?
quiz.explanation: Edge Impulse uses CMSIS-NN optimized kernels for ARM Cortex-M (< 10 ms typical) and its board profiler reports exact RAM/Flash usage before deployment, taking the guesswork out of fitting a model.
